text: FINO (CAPE), a long rocky promontory on the coast of Genoa, 3 m. WNW of Porto-Fino, in N lat. 44° 19', E long. 9° 14½'. The port is about 140 fath. long, and 70 fath. wide, with 9 fath. water at its mouth.
FINO, a small island in the Baltic, near the E coast of Sweden, in N lat. 58° 9'. FIO
FINO, a small town of Lombardy, 5 m. S of Como. Pop. 1,000.
FINOE, a parish in co. Tipperary, 3 m. WSW of Borris-o'-Kane. Area 5,053 acres. Pop. 1,576.

FINOW, a small river of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, which is formed by the union of 4 small streams near Biesenthal, and falls into the Oder. The Oder and the Havel are connected by a canal of very old date by means of this river.
FINOW, a town of Prussia, in the Ucker-Mark of Brandenburg, 32 m. NE of Berlin, and 28 m. NW of Custrin, on the l. bank of the Fühne. Pop. 560.
FINOW (Lower), a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, E of Eberswalde. Pop. 477.
FINS, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Allier, com. of Chatillon. Large coal mines are wrought here. Pop. 451.
FINSERWOLDE, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Groningen, 5 m. NW of Winschoten. Pop.
FINSBURY. See LONDON.

FINSTER-AARHORN, a mountain of Switzerland, in the group of the Bernese Alps, under the parallel of 46° 32'. It is a sharp needle-formed summit, starting up from the centre of the Unter-Aar glacier, to an alt. of 14,106 ft. above sea-level. It is the highest mountain in Switzerland.
FINSTERBERG, a mountain of Prussian Saxony, in the Thuringerwald. Alt. 2,941 ft. above sea-level.

FINSTERMUNZ, a remarkable defile in the Rheof the prov.; the Tana, which forms the boundary | tian Alps, between Tyrol and the Grisons, 16 m. N of Glurenz. It has an alt. above sea-level of 2,992 ft.; and is traversed by the river Inn.
FINSTERWALDE, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, circle of Luckau, 36 m. N of Dresden, on the l. bank of the Shakebach. Pop. 4,680.
FINTHEN, a town in the duchy of Hesse-Darmstadt, circle of Starkenburg, W of Mainz. Pop. 1,528.
FINTRAY, a parish of Aberdeenshire, skirting the N bank of the Don for about 6 m. Pop. in 1801, 886; in 1841, 1,032.

FINTRY, a parish near the centre of Stirlingshire. Its surface consists chiefly of hills, forming part of the range which stretches between Stirling and Dumbarton, and immediately N of the flanking summits called the Campsie-fells. The northern range, which is the broadest, is called the F.-hills. The only inhabited parts of the p. are two valleys, watered respectively by the Carron and the Endrick. Along the Carron is the commencement of the Carron-bog or meadow, probably the largest level of its class in Scotland. Beginning in F., it runs E between the ps. of Kilsyth and St. Ninians to the extent of 4 m., being in some places 2 m. in breadth, and in none less than 1 m. It comprehends an area of about 5,000 acres.—The v. of F. is delightfully situated on a rising ground along the side of the Endrick. Pop. of the v. 600; of the p. in 1801, 958; in 1831, 1,051; in 1841, 884.

FIONDA, or FIRONDA, a village of Asia Minor, on the W coast of the gulf of Satalieh, 22 m. S by W of Adalia. It is called TEKROVA by the Turks. The remains of the ancient Phaselis are situated on a small peninsula at the foot of Mount Takhtalu [alt. 7,800 ft.], in the vicinity of this place. The three ports and lake described by Strabo may still be traced here. An interesting account of this place is given in Admiral Beaufort's Karamania.
FIONIA. See FUNEN.
FIORA, a small river of Central Italy, which rises near Santa Fiore, and falls into the Tuscan sea below Montalto, in the duchy of Castro, 20 m. NW of Civita-Vecchia, after a course of 40 m.
FIORANO (SAN), a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Lodi-et-Crema, 2 m. SSE of Codogno. Pop. 1,650.
FIORE (SANTA), a town of Tuscany, in the prov. and 45 m. SSE of Sienna.
FIORENTINA, a small town of Naples, in the Capitanata, 7 m. S of Lucera.
FIORENTINO, a town of the Papal states, in the Campagna-di-Roma, 23 m. N of Terracina, and 18 m. E of Veletri.
FIORENZO. See FLORENT (SAINT).
FIRAKA, or Phing-Lou, one of the 12 districts of the prov. of Dewa, island of Nifon, Japan.
FIRAN, a small island of the Red sea, 18 m. from the coast of Yemen. It belongs to the principality of Abu-Arish, and is 54 m. NW of the town of that name. It has a pearl-fishery.

FIRANDO, or PHING-HOU, an island of Japan, prov. of Fisen, near the NE coast of the island of Kiusiu, in N lat. 33° 30', E long. 129° 38'. It is 27 m. in length, and about 15 m. in breadth. It has a town of the same name, with a good harbour, on the E coast, 72 m. NNW of Nagasaki. A factory was established here by the Dutch in 1609, but in 1640 it was abandoned in favour of Nagasaki.
FIRAO, or Fairao, a considerable town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Yamato, 45 m. SSE of Meaco.
FIRAYAKI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Dewa, 150 m. NW of Nambou.
FIRBANK, a chapelry in the p. and 10 m. N of Kirkby Lonsdale, Westmoreland, bounded on the W by the Lune. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 199.
FIRBECK, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SSW of Tickhill. Area 1,330 acres. Pop. 191.
FIRBY, a township in the p. of Weston, E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 490 acres. Pop. 36.—Also a township in the p. of Bedale, N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 530 acres. Pop. 54.
FIRENZE. See FLORENCE.

FIRENZUOLA, or FIORENZUELA, a town of the duchy of Parma, in the district and 7 m. WNW of Borgo-San-Donnino, and 17 m. ESE of Piacenza, in a fine plain, on the r. bank of the Lardi. Pop. 3,000. It has an abbey, a public library, and a classical academy; and is noted as the birth-place of Cardinal Alberoni. About 9 m. S of this town are the ruins, lately excavated, of the ancient Veleia. lages.
FIREPLACE, a village of Brookhaven township, Suffolk co., in the state of New York, U. S., 212 m. SE of Albany, on the N side of Fireplace bay. Pop. in 1840, 125.

FIRES (BAY OF), an indentation of the E coast of Van Diemen's Land, between Points Eddystone and St. Helena, in S lat. 41° 10′, E long. 148° 15′. It is semicircular in form, and is about 11 m. in diameter from N to S. George's river forms an extensive estuary on its S side.

FIRIA, a kingdom of Senegambia, bounded on the N by the kingdom of Amana; on the E by that of Sangara; on the S by the kingdom of Sulimana; on the SW by the kingdoms of Timisso and Tuta Jallon; and on the NW by the kingdom of Baleya. It is intersected by an extensive range of mountains, and watered by several of the head-streams of the Niger.
FIRIANGOMBI, a river of Lower Guinea, in 1,167. Congo, which takes its rise in S lat. 5° 40', and flows into the Zaire on the l. bank, at Lembo, after a course of about 105 m.
FIRKEEL, a village of co. Cork. Area 5 acres. Pop. 153.
FIRLE (West), a parish in Sussex, 43 m. ESE of Lewes. Area 4,410 acres. Pop. 722.
FIRLEY, a town of Poland, in the woiwodie, obwodie, and 26 m. N of Lublin. Pop. 335.
FIRMI, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Aubin, 21 m. NE of Ville- FIS franche-de-Rourjue. Pop. in 1841, 1,572. It has several blast-furnaces. Coal is wrought here.

FIRMIN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Senlis. Pop. 1,152.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Montcenis. Pop. 556. It has several blast-furnaces and iron-forges.

FIRMIN-EN-VALGODEMARD (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Alpes, arrond. of Gap.—The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,496; in 1841, 5,394.—The village is in the valley of Godemard, near the r. bank of the Sevraysse. Pop. 1,168. It has some manufactories of woollen fabrics. Marble of fine quality is found in the vicinity.

FIRMINY, or FIRMINI, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Le Chambon, 8 m. SW of Saint-Etienne. Pop. 4,306. It has extensive iron, steel, and glass-works, and several silk-mills and manufactories of ribbon. Coal is abundant in the environs.
FIRMO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 7 m. SSW of Castrovillari, on the r. bank of the Tyro. Pop. 935. It has a Greek church.
FIRMONTH, the highest mountain in the forest of Glentanner, in Aberdeenshire. It is elevated about 2,500 ft. above the level of the sea.
FIRMY, a town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. and 3 m. ENE of Saint-Aubin. Pop. 1,050. Copper and coal are found in the environs.
FIRNSBY, or FIRENSBY, a township in the p. of Farnham, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 400 acres. Pop. 112.

FIROZABAD, a town of Hindostan, prov. of Agra, situated on the E side of the Jumna, 12 m. WNW of Shekohabad. It is enclosed with a mud wall, and has towers at the angles.—Also a town of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, 60 m. S by E of Shiraz.

FIROZEH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi. As three of the Afghan emperors of Hindostan bore the title of Firoz, i.e. 'the victorious,' a number of towns were called after them, of which many are now in ruins, and others reduced to vil-
FIROZKUH, a village of Persia, in Irak-Ajemi, on the route from Tehran to Astrabad. It lies at the foot of a remarkable cliff rising abruptly from the banks of the Hablasud.
FIROZPUR. See FEROZEPUR.
FIRSBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 4 m. SE of Spilsby. Area 910 acres. Pop. in 1831, 142; in 1841, 196.
FIRSBY (East), a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. WSW of Market-Raisen. Area 690 acres. Pop. in 1831, 29; in 1841, with the township of West F., 87.

FIRST SIGHT ISLAND, one of the Solomon islands, in the S. Pacific ocean, in S lat. 7° 15', E long. 149° 10'. It is about 6 m. in length, and was discovered by M. Surville in 1769. It was then uninhabited, and covered with fruit-trees.
FIRTH, a parish on the mainland of Orkney, stretching across the island from the sound of Hoy to that of Damsay. Pop. in 1831, 1,200; in 1841,
FIRUZKOH, a fortress of Bukhara, on the mountains which separate Balkh from Sistan. It was taken in 1404 by Tamerlane.

FISCHA, a river of Lower Austria, which rises in the Schneeberg, and falls into the Danube at Fischamund, 10 m. ESE of Vienna, after a course from SW to NE of 24 m.-Also a river of Lower Austria, which falls into the Leytha near Lichtenberg. It is distinguished as the Small F.
FISCHAMUND, a small town of Lower Austria, on the Gross Fischa, 12 m. ESE of Vienna. Pop. 1 1,820. A magnificent view of the vale of the Danube may be obtained from the neighbourhood of this place.
FISCHAU, a town of Prussia, in the gov. of Danzig, NE of Marienburg. Pop. 400.

FISCHBACH, a river of Bavaria, which rises near Kaiserslautern, and running W, joins the Schwartzbach near Wald-Fischbach, where the confluent streams receive the name of the Erbach.—Also a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Pfalz, SE of Pirmasens. Pop. 576.—Also a town of Prussian Silesia, in the circle of Leignitz, SE of Hirschberg. Pop. 1,286.—Also a village of Hesse, 21 m. NE of Marburg.
FISCHEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, 20 m. S of Kempten, on the l. bank of the Iller. Pop. 1,400.

FISCHENTHAL, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 20 m. E of Zurich, bail. and 10 m. NE of Grünengen, near the l. bank of the Toss, and at the foot of Mont Almann. It has a church, founded in the 9th century.

FISCHHAUSEN, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. and 24 m. W of Königsberg, on the N side of the Trischen-Haff. Pop. in 1837, 1,644. Leather forms the chief article of local manufacture. Pop. of circle, 28,800.

FISCHINGEN, a village of Switzerland, in the r. bank of the Murg. It has an abbey with a handsome church and a library.—Also a village of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, bail. of Lorrach. Pop. 327. It has a mineral spring.
FISCIANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato-Citra, district and 8 m. NNE of Salerno, and 2 m. ESE of Severino. Pop. 1,075. It has two parish-churches and the same number of convents.

FISEN, a province of Japan, consisting of the NW portion of the island of Kiusiu, bounded on the N by the strait of Corea, on the E by the prov. of Tsikouzen and Tsikovogo, and on the S and W by the sea of Japan. It comprises the peninsulas of Omoura and Naganaski.

FISH RIVER (GREAT), the most considerable river of S. Africa, rising beyond the Sneeberg and under the parallel of 32°, and flowing first E and then S to its junction with the Tarka, 10 m. S of Cradock; then crossing the district of Somerset in a winding course from N to S, and receiving the Baviaans river on its l. bank, and the Little Fish river on its r.; then turning E from the junction of the latter river, and winding across the N part of Albany, receiving the frontier river of that prov., the Kunap, on its l. bank, at Kunap-fort, and the Kat river 10 m. below; then turning SSE, and separating, throughout the remainder of its course, the newly ceded territory on the E from Albany on the W, and flowing into the ocean in lat. 33° 47', after a course of at least 400 m. by the windings of its stream, though not above 150 m. from its source. On each side of its mouth is a wild, rocky, and open shore; but the projecting cheeks form a small cove or creek. The bar has been crossed by a boat, but it is constantly shifting, and the offing is more exposed than the mouth of the Cowie. When fairly into the river, there is a breadth of from 600 to 700 yds. for nearly 7 m. up. The first ford across the river is at Caffre's Drift, 30 m. from the sea; but the tide flows to Sand Drift some distance beyond this point. There are numerous drifts, or fords, above this point, in which the ordinary depth varies from 2 to 4 ft., but sometimes in dry weather does not exceed a few inches. After heavy rains, the stream rises rapidly, and the passage becomes very formidable. "I have crossed the F. river," says a recent FIS writer, "when the water scarcely reached my horse's knees; and returning to the same drift three days afterwards, have found it nearly 30 ft. deep. In 1819, the Great F. rose 70 ft. above its ordinary level! The banks of this river are generally lofty, massy, and rampart-like, sloping rapidly down to its narrow valley, which is generally enveloped in dark bush, through which parallel strata of grey rock gleam forth like successive ranges of battlements. Through these banks the descents leading down to the drifts on both sides are generally narrow deeplyworn passes. The actual channel of the river is narrow, and filled in many places with massy fragments of rocks. The course of the Great F., from a little below De Bruin's drift to a few miles from its mouth, runs through a jungle more or less dense, the mean breadth of which is about 5 m., and its length, following the windings of the river, not under 130 m. It occupies both banks, which, as already remarked, are broken into deep ravines and gulleys, and in some parts precipitous. The height of the jungle is about 12 ft., and it is chiefly composed of Euphorbia Speckboom (Portulacoria Afra), and a great variety of other succulent shrubs and bushes, which at no season of the year are capable of being set on fire. Its density is variable. It was some years since much frequented by elephants and buffaloes, which made numerous paths in it in all direccant. of Thurgau, 10 m. SSE of Frauenfeld, on the tions; since the district has become more populous, numerous bridle-paths, and some waggon-roads, have been cut through it, especially from one military post to another. Generally speaking, the Great F. bush is pervious to cattle, to men on foot, and in many places to horsemen; the Caffres pass through it with ease and rapidity, but there are many parts of it which would present great difficulties even to them. Near De Bruin's drift there is only a separation of a few miles between the bush and the Zuuzberg mountains, which are also covered with jungle of the same description, and are connected with the Sunday River bush; so that the whole occupies a vast space, and extends within a few hours of Uitenhage." Mr. Banbury, describing the bush along this stream, says: "I never saw, in any other part of the world, any resembling the Fish River bush; nor, I should think, does there exist a tract so difficult to penetrate or to clear. The vegetation is so succulent that fire has no effect on it, even in the driest weather; and at the same time so strong and rigid, and so excessively dense, that there is no getting through it without cutting your way at every step, unless in the paths made by wild beasts. Yet the Caffres make their way through with wonderful skill and activity, creeping like snakes among the thickets, where no white man can follow them; and this covert, extending so far along the frontier, is of great advantage to them, both in their predatory and hostile incursions, as they can muster in force, and even approach to within a few miles of Graham's Town, without being observed."

FISH RIVER (GREAT), or THLEW-EE-CHOH, a large river of Arctic America, rising, under the parallel of 64° 52', in Sussex lake, which is separated from Lake Aylmer to the S by a narrow ridge, part of a continuous height of land which extends from the borders of the Coppermine river. The portage from Lake Aylmer, to its outlet from Sussex lake, is short of a mile; and the height of the dividing land between the waters flowing S to Hudson's bay, and those flowing N to the Arctic ocean, is short of 2 ft. From Sussex lake it flows NNE to Lake Beechey; under the parallel of 65° 40'; where it issues in a series of cascades, falling 60 ft. in 2 m. distance, and flows ESE to the junction with Baillie's river on the r. bank, beneath which its stream widens considerably, and turns again NE with a breadth varying from a 1 m. to 1 m.; under the parallel of 66° it expands into Lake Pelly, and issuing from the E side of that lake, flows successively through Lake Garry, and Lake Macdougall, and in a series of winding and intricate reaches, to Sinclair's falls, below which it is 1 m. broad. It then turns again NE, and finally pours its waters into the Polar sea, in N lat. 67° 11', W long. 94° 30', after a violent and tortuous course of 530 geog. m., through an iron-ribbed country, without a single tree on the whole line of its banks, and after having descended through no less than 83 falls, cascades, and rapids. The exploration of this river is due to the energy, skill, and perseverance of Captain Back.

FISH RIVER, a river of New South Wales, which rises near the SW confines of Westmoreland co., runs N, bends along the N border of the co., and joins the Macquarie at the point of junction of Bathurst and Roxburgh counties.
FISHBOURNE (New), a parish in Sussex, 14 m. W of Chichester. Area 610 acres. Pop. 295.
FISHBURN, a township in the p. of Sedgefield, co. and 91 m. SSE of Durham. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. in 1831, 212; in 1841, 239.
FISHER, a hamlet in the p. of North Mundham, Sussex. Pop. in 1841, 61.

FISHER-SOUND, a channel of British North America, which forms a continuation of Fitzhugh sound, and separates the Princess Royal islands from the continent in N lat. 52°, and W long. 130°. It was discovered by Vancouver in 1793.
FISHER'S ISLAND, an island of the United States, near the NE extremity of Long Island, and 14 m. from the shore of the state of Connecticut. It is 8 m. long, and about 1 m. in breadth.
FISHER'S ISLAND. See Pescadores.
FISHER'S, or Pecheur's Island. See WISHART ISLAND.
FISHERTON-ANGER, a parish in Wilts, 4 m. W of Salisbury, from which it is separated by the Avon. Area 660 acres. Pop. 1,739.
FISHERTON-DE-LA-MERE, a parish in Wilts, 10 m. SW of Amesbury, on the Wiley. Area 3,300 acres. Pop. 324.
FISHERWICK, a township in the p. of St. Michael, Lichfield, Staffordshire, 31 m. NE of Lichfield, on the Tame. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. 86.

FISHGUARD, a parish, sea-port town, and borough of Pembrokeshire, 4 m. N of Haverfordwest. Pop. of p., in 1831, 1,990; in 1841, 2,013. The town is situated on a cliff, at the mouth of the Gwayne. It is irregularly built, and the streets are very steep. The port is capable of containing 100 ships, about which number belong to the port. Improvements, consisting of a breakwater near the rocks called the Cow and Calf, a pier to extend from the fort point, and an inner basin or basins, with harbour-lights and light-houses, the expense of which has been estimated at £250,000, are expected to constitute this port an asylum-harbour for vessels of all descriptions navigating St. George's channel: the anchorage being good, the ground holding well, and the bay having 2,604. depth of water for the largest vessels at all times of tide. There is excellent herring and other fishing ground near the bay, frequented by a number of small craft from Liverpool. Turbot and john-dory are taken in great quantities; salmon also are caught. Butter, oats, and slates are exported. The imports are culm from Milford-haven, coals from various quarters, and general merchandise. A detachment of 800 French troops effected a landing here in 1797, and were made prisoners. F. is contributory to the borough of Haverford-West, and is a polling-place for the county elections. FIT
FISH-HOUSE, a village of Northampton township, Fulton co., in the state of New York, U. S., on Sacandaga river, a branch of the Hudson. Pop. in 1840, 250.

FISHING CREEK, a village of Cape May co., in the state of New Jersey, U.S., on a small creek of the same name, an affluent of Delaware bay.—Also a township of Columbia co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 101 m. NE of Harrisburg. It has a mountainous surface, and is watered by Fishing creek. Its soil consists of clay and gravel. Pop. 904.
FISHING RIVER, a township of Ray co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 1,845.

FISHKILL, a township of Duchess co., in the state of New York, U.S., 88 m.S of Albany. It is generally hilly, and is watered by Wappinger's and Fishkill creeks, both of which flow into Hudson's river, on the W confines. Pop. 10,437. The central village is on Fishkill creek, 5 m. E of Fishkill Landing. Pop. 800.
FISHKILL LANDING, a village of Fishkill township, Duchess co., in the state of New York, U. S., 90 m. S of Albany, at the confluence of Fishkill creek with Hudson river. Pop. in 1840, 900.

FISHKILL MOUNTAINS, called also the Highlands of Hudson river, or Matteawan mountains, a branch of the Alleghany chain, about 40 m. above New York, and extending nearly 20 m. along both sides of the Hudson. See Alleghany.
FISHLAKE, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, on the river Don, 2½ m. W of Thorne. Area 6,170 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,334; in 1841, 1,257.
FISHTOWN, a large native town on the W coast of Africa, at the mouth of a river of that name, to the SW of Cape Palmas.
FISHWICK, a township in the p. and 13 m. E of Preston, in Lancashire. Pop. 756.
FISKERTON, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. E of Lincoln. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. in 1831, 330; in 1841, 410.—Also a p. in Notts, 3 m. SE of Southwell. Area 570 acres. Pop. in 1831, 304; in 1841, 402.
FISKERNAES, a Danish settlement, founded in 1754, on the W coast of Greenland, in N lat. 63°, at the mouth of the Fisker river.

FISMES, or Fimes, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Marne, arrond. of Rheims.—The cant., comprising 23 com., had a pop. of 12,724 in 1841.— The town, 14 m. W of Rheims, is a place of considerable antiquity, and was the seat of two ecclesiastical councils, in the 9th and 10th cents. Pop. in 1841, 2,366.
FISSATA, a small seaport of Tripoli, 90 m. NW of Tripoli.
FISTRITZ. See BISTRITZ.

FITATS, a prov. of Japan, on the E coast of the isle of Nifon. It is a mountainous district, and is watered by several rivers. Silk and cattle are its chief productions. Its cap. is Mito; but it has a town of the same name, on the banks of a small river, 108 m. ENE of Jedo.
FITCHBURG, a township of Massachusetts, U. S., 38 m. NW of Boston, in Worcester co. Pop.
FITCHVILLE, a township in Huron co., in Ohio, U. S., 98 m. N by E of Columbus. Pop. 1,292.
FITERO, a town of Spain, in Navarre, 12 m. WSW of Tudela, near the l. bank of the Alhama. It is celebrated for its hot springs.

FITFUL-HEAD, a promontory on the W of Quendal-bay, the most southerly part of Shetland. It is a bold and extensive headland, consisting of a large assemblage of strata of clay slate, rising 400 ft. perpendicularly out of the ocean, and seen at a great distance by vessels approaching from the SW. At Gauhsness near Fitful-head, occurs a vein or perhaps bed of iron-pyrites which was a few years ago unsuccessfully wrought for the purpose of finding copper-ore, whilst many hundred tons of iron-pyrites were thrown into the sea.
FITILIEU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Pont-de-Beauvoisin. Pop. 1,433.
FITTAH, the cap. of the Russian prov. of Shirvan. It became the cap, of the prov. in the early part of the present century, when Shamekhia was laid in ruins. Pop. 28,000.
FITTLETON, a parish in Wilts, 8 m. W of Ludgershall. Area 3,050 acres. Pop. in 1831, 331; in 1841, 346.
FITTLEWORTH, a parish in Sussex, 3 m. SE of Petworth. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. in 1831, 668; in 1841, 713.
FITTON (Mount), a peak in which the E range of the Rocky mountains terminates, on the shores of the Arctic sea.
FITTON POINT, a promontory on the coast of the Arctic sea, in N lat. 70° 11', W long. 126° 15'.

FITTRE, or FIDDRI, a large lake of Central Africa, situated in a small kingdom of the same name, called also Lussi, Cougu, and Darkuka, in N lat. 13° 45', E long. 20° 30', about 200 m. E of Lake Chad. The territory of F. was formerly more extensive, but has been much reduced in war with Beghermi. It is governed, however, by a sultan of its own. The inhabitants live in small huts, and are said to be in a low state of civilisation. They have no salt except what they procure from the ashes of a plant, called the gassab.—The lake is four days' journey in circumf. during the dry season; but in the wet season increases to double that extent. It is understood to be the same called Cauga by Edrisi, in the 11th cent. That writer also seems to represent it as having a communication with the Niger.—Hornemann.
FITZ, a parish in Salop, 4 m. NNW of Shrewsbury. Area 1,670 acres. Pop. in 1831, 211; in 1841, 244.

FITZABADING, a district of Abyssinia, stretching along the E side of the Bir river, and intersected by the parallel of 10° 30' N lat. It is a pleasant fertile district, presenting the features rather of an English than an African landscape.—Beke.
FITZGIBBON (Point), a cape on the W coast of North America, in N lat. 55° 55'.
FITZHEAD, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. ENE of Wiveliscombe. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 339.
FITZHERBERT'S ISLAND, a small island in the Florida stream, at the entrance of the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 24° 40'.
FITZHUGH'S SOUND, a narrow channel of the N. Pacific, between Calvert's island and the W coast of North America, in N lat. 51° 33'.
FITZJAMES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. and 3 m. NE of Clermont. Pop. 484. It was erected into a ducal peerage in 1710, in favour of the Duke of Berwick.

FITZROY, a township of Upper Canada, in Dalhousie district, intersected by the Mississippi river on the W. Pop. in 1842, 1,746.—The v. of F. is situated on a bay of the Ottawa river in this township. Pop. 500.—Also a river of SE. Australia, flowing in a SE course into Portland bay.—Also a river of N. Australia, flowing into the head of King Sound. Its course has been traced up to S lat. 17° 44', E long. 124° 34', a distance of 90 m. from the coast-line, above which it appears to form a series of deep reaches connected by shallows.
FITZROY DOWNS, a district of Tropical Australia, in S lat. 26° 35', E long. 148° 40', intersected by Grafton range, and skirted on the W by the Maranoa river.
FITZROY ISLAND, a small island near the NE FIV coast of New Holland, 5 m. NE of Cape Grafton, in S lat. 16° 51′ 20″, E long. 145° 53′ 5″.
FITZWILLIAM, a township in Cheshire co., in New Hampshire, U. S., 60 m. SW of Concord. Pop. 1,366.

FIUME, a seaport of the Austrian empire, in Croatia, at the mouth of the Fiumara, which flows into the extremity of the gulf of Quarnero, 40 m. SE of Trieste. It consists of two parts, -an inner town, which is old and narrow, and an outer town, which is new and well-built, along the sea-side. The harbour, though somewhat difficult of entrance, is commodious; and large vessels may ride safely at anchor at some distance. F. communicates with Carlstadt and with Zeng by the Carolina and Josephina roads. Unfortunately the tolls are high, and the Save and Culpa are ill-navigated, so that the trade of F., though possessing the advantages of a free port, is almost restricted to the local resources of ship-building, for which the neighbouring chain of the Julian Alps affords excellent wood. Rags, staves, corn, and tobacco, are exported; but the best part of the trade is with smugglers. The exports of Hungary through F., on an average of 7 years, are only £200,000 per ann. In the neighbourhood of the town is a paper-mill belonging to an English company. The pop. of the town is about 9,000, speaking indifferently Italian and Slavic, with a sprinkling of Magyar functionaries. The landed property about F., and its dependencies, consist of vineyards chiefly, none of which exceed 40 acres in extent, and the greater part are very small. Maize is the principal grain cultivated, and is sown in rows between the vines. The local municipality collect a tax of one karantan per brava, or about 13 farthing per 56.16 sq. ft., from the proprietors of the soil. The fuel in use is brought from Istria, from islands in the vicinity, and from the interior.— Ever since 1471, F. has belonged to the house of Hapsburg. In 1530 it received municipal institutions from Charles V. In 1776 Maria Theresa incorporated it with Hungary. During the French empire, it formed part of Illyria. See HUNGARIAN LITTORALE.

FIUME-FREDDO, a river of Sicily, in the Valdi-Demona, which flows into the sea at Aci-Reale. It has a town of the same name on its banks, with a pop. of 2,840.—Also a town of Naples, in Calabria- Citra, 8 m. S of Paola. Pop. 1,000.
FIUME-GRANDE, a river of Sicily, which forms the E boundary of the Val-di-Mazzara. It is the ancient Himera Septentrionalis.
FIUME-SALSO, a large river of Sicily, which rises in the Madonie mountains, and empties itself into the sea at Alicata. Formerly it bore the name of Himera Meridionalis.

FIUMICINO, a small seaport of the Papal states, at the mouth of the W or lesser branch of the Tiber, 2 m. SW of Porto, 17 m. from Rome. The channel of the river here requires to be repeatedly cleared of the accumulated sand. The current is strong, and the entrance so narrow as to be dangerous even to large boats. Near it is a fortified tower, with a small garrison. The chief supply of fish for Rome is obtained from this place.
FIVE BONNETS, a cluster of small islands in the Mergui archipelago, in N lat. 10° 29'.
FIVE BROTHERS, a cluster of small islands in Lake Huron, in N lat. 44° 55'.

FIVE-FINGERS-POINT, a cape on the W coast of New Zealand, forming the N promontory of the S entrance of Dusky bay, in S lat. 45° 33', E long. 166° 18'. Several pointed rocks lying off it, when viewed from certain positions have some resemblance to the fingers of a man's hand.
FIVEHEAD, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. SW of Langport. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. in 1831, 387; in 1841, 412.
FIVE-HUMMOCKS'-POINT, a conspicuous projecting point of land on the coast of Lower California, in N lat. 30° 24'.
FIVE ISLANDS, a cluster of small islands in the Mergui archipelago, in N lat. 10° 13'.
FIVE-ISLANDS-HARBOUR, a bay on the W coast of the island of Antigua, in N lat. 17° 13'.
FIVEL, a small river of Holland, which runs into the Ems near Delfzyl. It gives name to a district on its banks, between Gröningen and the mouth of the Ems.
FIVE-MILE-BRIDGE, a hamlet in the p. of Carrigaline, co. Cork.
FIVE-MILE-POINT, a headland and fishingvillage in co. Wicklow, 5½ m. N of Wicklow.
FIVE-MILE-TOWN, a town in the p. of Clogher, co. Tyrone, 41 m. NE of Brookborough. Pop. 693.
FIVES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Lille. Pop. 1,520.
FIVE STAKES, a village of Upper Canada, in the township of Southwold, 3 m. from St. Thomas. Pop. 100.
FIVIZZANO, a small town of Tuscany, near the E frontier of the duchy of Genoa, 13 m. NNE of Carrara, and 18 m. ENE of Spezia, on the l. bank of the Rosaro. Pop. 1,800.
FIXIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Gevrey. Pop. 414.
FIXBY, a township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. NNW of Huddersfield. Pop. in 1831, 348; in 1841, 399.
FIZE-FONTAINE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Bodegnée. Pop. 498.
FIZE-LE-MARSAL, a village of Belgium, in the 150. prov. of Liege, cant. of Momalle. Pop. 286.
FLAACH, a parish and village of the Swiss cant. of Zurich, at the conflux of the Rhine and Thur, 8 m. SW of Schaffhausen. Pop. 1,536.
FLAARDING, a small neat town of S. Holland, on the Maese, 5 m. W by S of Rotterdam, inhabited chiefly by herring-fishers.
FLACEY-EN-BRESSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Cusseaux. Pop. 1,000.
FLADBURY, a parish in Worcestershire, 3½ m. E of Pershore. Area 7,830 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,407; in 1841, 1,448.
FLADDA, an island of the Hebrides, off the NE coast of Skye.—Also one of the Treishnish isles, near the island of Mull, in Argyleshire.
FLADDACHUAIN, an islet off the NW point of Skye, about 3 m. in length, and 300 yds. in average breadth.
FLADSTRAND, a small but fortified seaport of Denmark, in Jutland, 30 m. NNE of Aalborg, in N lat. 57° 27′ 3″.
